raw_text:
```json
{
  "claims": [
    "University of California campuses will face two additional state audits due to slow progress in returning wrongly held Native American human remains.",
    "Federal and state law required UC to return Native American human remains and items by 1995.",
    "Minimal progress has been achieved by the UC campuses to meet requirements of the Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act (NAGPRA) and California Native American Graves Protection and Repatriation Act (CalNAGPRA) despite two critical state audits.",
    "The 2020 UC audit reported that Berkeley held an inventory of 496,500 remains and artifacts and had repatriated 19 percent of that inventory.",
    "The 2020 UC audit reported that Los Angeles possessed 58,200 remains and artifacts, repatriating 96 percent of them.",
    "The 2020 UC audit reported that Davis had only returned two percent of its collections, which numbered 46,500.",
    "The 2022 UC audit revealed that the Office of the President had not prioritized returning remains and cultural items to the tribes.",
    "Berkeley had repatriated 29 percent of its collections as reported in the 2021 audit review.",
    "Davis had repatriated 95 percent of its collections as reported in the 2021 audit review.",
    "Los Angeles had repatriated 99 percent of its collections as reported in the 2021 audit review.",
    "Riverside and San Diego had repatriated less than one percent of their collections as reported in the 2021 audit review.",
    "AB 389 aims to address the California State University system's failure to return almost 700,000 Native American remains and cultural items."
  ]
}
```
